All right. Welcome back. Let's move forward here. And I want to revert back for a moment to the subject of handshakes in terms of that your best handshake for getting through a poor is the standard co-operators handshake, where you basically meet your prospects hand-on and return whatever pressure they gave you in kind. You're not above them. You're not below them. You're exactly even with them. Well, the reason this handshake is so effective is it adapts into a very powerful rapport-building strategy called matching, which means that you basically enter or match your prospects world where they are. In other words, in this context, matching would simply mean that if someone shakes your hand firmly, you want to shake their hand just as firmly. But again, not too firmly. You want to get that old Mexican squeeze off, right? But essentially by adopting the same physiology as your prospect, it allows you to effortlessly slide through rapport with them. Some other examples are the position of your prospects body, their posture, their rate of breathing, even how fast they blink. All of these things can be matched. See, the bottom line is that matching is an incredibly powerful tool for getting into rapport, especially when you're in person and you can match both body language and tonality. But it's also extremely effective on the phone too. You focus on just matching someone's tonality and also their rate of speech, the types of words they use, the slang. And finally, before we think of something creepy here, and it's much as that you're copying someone, let me clarify that you're not actually copying them. You're matching them. And there's a very big difference. You see, copying someone is called mirroring, meaning that you actually try to mirror your prospects physical actions in real time as they're doing them. In other words, if they scratch their nose, you scratch your nose. They cross their legs, lean back in their seat, then you cross your legs and lean back in your seat. Now, that is creepy. And it's also really obvious. You see why I'm not a big fan of it. No, in fact, to tell you a story, I've been in situations where people have tried to actually mirror me to get into rapport. It's the craziest thing. I was in a meeting and some guy is scratching, I know I scratched my nose because of scratching. I'm like, what the heck, right? I lean back, he leans back. I'm like, buddy, are you actually trying to mirror me? And he's like, no, no, I'm not. I'm like, all right, whatever. Then we start talking again. And I lean back to him and he leans to you. I'm like, dude, stop it. You get it? I mean, honestly, I think mirroring is something you should avoid at all costs. But I am a fan of matching, which means if your prospect leans back in their seat, then you lean back in your seat as well, but you do it slowly, casually, with maybe a five or 10 second leg. So it's not blatantly obvious. See in the end, it all goes back to that rapport building concept of likability, meaning that people want to do business with other people who were essentially like them as opposed to different. And when it comes to enhancing your likability through unconscious communication, the way you start this process is by entering your prospect's world where they are. And then you pace them, you pace them, and then you lead them in the direction that you want them to go. Now the actual name of this strategy is called pacing and leading. Although I like to refer to it as pace, pace, lead. Because you pace them, you pace them, and you lead them where you want them to go. Now the word pacing means that you essentially match the physiology or tone of your prospect in terms of how they're actually holding their body at the time or what tone they're speaking in. But then after a short time, you switch into leading them, which is where you start to actually shift your tone or physiology to the direction you want your prospect to go. Now with the straight line system, we use this with amazing effect, especially in the back half of the sale in the looping process. We want to build both logical and emotional certainty at the same time. So what I'm going to do here is actually teach this to you in two chunks. I'll explain the overall concept to you here in this module. And then once you understand how it works, when we get to the module on looping, I'll take it through a real world example of pace, pace, lead and show you how we execute this syllable by syllable. Really powerful stuff. So that being said, the first thing to remember here is that there's pace, pace, lead and pace, pace, freaking lead, which is the way I teach, which is basically ninja style on steroids. What I mean by that is that pacing and leading is one of those strategies that needs to be executed exactly right, or it simply doesn't work. However, when you do get it right, and it's not that hard to do by the way, watch out. I mean, not only are you able to increase your prospect's level of emotional certainty, but you'll also be able to do, that's not even realizing it. In other words, pace, pace, lead flies under the radar of the conscious mind and hits your prospect straight in the gut. In fact, that would be a great example right here, telling you a story about my son Carter, right? Back in the day when he was 11 years old. So here's the deal. Carter was a great soccer player growing up. He played in the great club team, right? And there was one kid on his club team that drove him absolutely mad. His name was Andreas, right? He was a pusher of Warhawk. Anyway, I come home from work one day and my girl from that, she goes, oh my God, your son, he is so upset right now. He's freaking out. He's down to say he's actually livid. He was being pushed around by Andreas, right? And he's beside himself. He wants to quit the team. He wants to file a lawsuit. He's like flipping out, right? Now what you need to understand about my son Carter is that he's not that type of kid. I mean, he never really gets mad, loses his temper. He's always very calm, but this kid, Andreas, really got under his skin and he just lost it. I mean, so my fiance says to me, she goes, why don't you go down there and see if you can calm him down a bit? I hate to see him like that. So I go downstairs and me, the master communicator, tried to be as sympathetic in his comments. I go, what is possible? I drop my tone and I say, listen, buddy, I know you're really upset right now, but you shouldn't let someone get to you this way. It's just not good for you. And my son's like, what do you mean? It's easy for you to say, you know, you have to deal with this idiot each week. I'm so angry. I can't take it anymore. I'm on a quick team and I'm like, whoa, Carter, I'm gonna calm down, buddy. You gotta calm down. No, I'm not going to calm down. I can't take this. He's a total bull. I hate to get everyone's ways. We want to throw him off the team. And I'm like, all right, Carter, calm down. No, we didn't. Now at which point Carter lost his shit completely, right? Well, let me ask you a question. Knowing what I know about communication, do you think that's actually what I did? In other words, I go downstairs, my son is spitting nails angry. Do you think that I actually go down there being calm? Of course not. You see, that's called answering someone's world where they're not. Someone's really upset and you come down calm as a cucumber. They're gonna get even more angry. So what I did here, something very powerful, I used the technology of pace, pace lead. I walked downstairs knowing that my son is absolutely living and I go downstairs even more live. I'm like, Carter, what's going on? I heard this, this kid addressed. He's such a bull. I hate that kid. I'm gonna get that kid through off the team. And Carter's like, yeah, he's a real idiot. This kid, I'm like, exactly. This kid's a menace. I swear to God. I don't know what we're gonna do. I just wonder if there's something wrong with them. It's almost like you gotta feel bad for them, right? And Carter's like, yeah, I guess you do. I'm like, yeah, it's just like, wow, when someone has got that many emotional problems, you have to just almost have some sympathy. You know what I mean? He goes, yeah, dad, I guess you're right. He's really laughing as I say this. I feel bad when he's playing with my son. That what happened was is by me getting even angrier than Carter, essentially I entered his world where he was and I got more angry and more angry and suddenly I shift my anger into a state of absolute calm, of compassion, of sympathy. And of course, what does Carter do? He will tend to match me. And he comes down and he's like, you're right, dad. What was I thinking? And just like that, I moved my son from a state of absolute anger to one of calmness and patience. That's the power of pace, pace, lead when you do it exactly right, meaning you do it ninja style on steroids. So there's pace, pace, lead and there's pace, pace, lead. And that's what I want you to perfect, the latter one. Now out to be clear, just so you know, I didn't invent pace and leading. It's been around since the dawn of human communication. But my point is that all great communicators do this naturally without even thinking about it. But the key here is that anyone can learn it once they know the rules like you do now and you put a bit of practice into it. And again, this is an introduction to pace, pace, lead. When we get into the module on looping, I'm going to hand you the exact lab which has on a silver planet and then go through them syllable by syllable so you can excrete this stuff, that ninja level on steroids, which will go a really long way to getting you that point where you can close anyone who's closeable. That's the power of this strategy. And with that, it's time to move on to the next module here. But first, please go through a quiz here. There's a bunch of stuff in the resource center. There's a lot of intercoms to body language. Make sure you master this stuff. And when you're done, I'll meet you in the next module and we'll roll on forward. See you soon.
